summary: Joe Reis argues that the tech industry's obsession with speed and arbitrary deadlines has led to a cargo-cult adoption of Agile that sacrifices quality for output. He contends that slowing down to build quality into processes—through proper testing, data modeling, and established conventions—ultimately leads to faster long-term productivity and less technical debt.
key_points: The quality paradox: moving slower initially to achieve higher quality results in faster long-term delivery with fewer errors to fix
Agile and XOps have become ceremonial memes focused on delivery deadlines rather than the 'valuable' part of valuable software delivery
Technical debt accumulates when teams ship subpar work, creating a house of cards that developers know will eventually collapse
Using established conventions like relational modeling and Kimball/Data Vault reduces errors by providing standardized, predictable approaches
Quality investment requires intentional focus—organizations budgeting zero for quality will get exactly that
money_quotes: Agile and XOps have both become ceremonial memes. Being 'Agile' often means hitting delivery deadlines, even if it introduces error-prone and buggy features.
Every time you ship something subpar, you put one more chip on the debt pile. At some point, you'll either pay down those chips or burn the pile to the ground.
A policy of 'Quality - If Time Permits' will assure that no quality at all sneaks into the product.
It amazes me how often I see relational databases without a relational model, and even more amazed when I hear developers complain about duplicate data and update/delete side effects.
key_insight: Organizations must resist the pressure to ship quickly at all costs and instead invest in quality practices upfront, recognizing that the short-term slowdown pays dividends through reduced rework and technical debt.
